ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken tikka entry (FOODON_03302051); no specific pudina/mint variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific chicken tikka or pudina tikka entry. A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for chicken pudina tikka or chicken tikka as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chicken Pudina Tikka is approximately 200 g, consisting of around 6 pieces of marinated grilled chicken, as commonly served in Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Tikka (tandoori-style grilled marinated chicken) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted with mint marinade adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for tandoori/grilled chicken tikka used as the primary base. Pudina (mint) is a low-calorie herb marinade component that does not materially alter macronutrient profile at typical usage levels. Values represent grilled boneless chicken with yogurt-mint marinade, cooked weight basis. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with ICMR-NIN grilled chicken preparations and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken breast, grilled (FDC ID 171477) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Pudina Tikka. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous grilled/tandoori chicken preparations. Grilled chicken is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with negligible glycemic impact. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values for protein-dominant meat dishes in the range of 0–35. A GI of 30 is a conservative defensible estimate for this preparation. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 2.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Chicken Pudina Tikka contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (760 mg per 200 g serving), which represents about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparation when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of approximately 30 and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Chicken Pudina Tikka has minimal glycemic impact and is a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is not served with high-GI accompaniments such as naan or white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken Pudina Tikka provides lean grilled chicken with approximately 1.8 g saturated fat per 100 g, which is within acceptable limits for cardiovascular health; the mint and spice marinade contributes antioxidant compounds, and the grilling method avoids added frying fats, making this a heart-friendly protein choice when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Pudina Tikka is an Indian grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a vibrant blend of fresh mint (pudina), yogurt, ginger, garlic, green chilli, lemon juice, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, and garam masala, then cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) or over a charcoal grill until lightly charred and juicy. The dish is a mint-forward variation of the classic Chicken Tikka, distinguished by the cooling, herbaceous freshness of pudina that balances the smoky heat of the tandoor. The texture is tender and slightly charred on the exterior, with a moist interior. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 25 g protein and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-GI and high-protein dietary patterns. The sodium content from the marinade is moderate at around 380 mg per 100 g, which warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~30) and is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. It is commonly served as a starter or appetiser in Indian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent, often accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-friendly.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chicken Pudina Tikka?
Is Chicken Pudina Tikka good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists specifically for Chicken Pudina Tikka. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN grilled/tandoori chicken tikka data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central grilled chicken breast values. The mint marinade does not materially alter macronutrient composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant grilled chicken preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero for lean grilled chicken preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA data. All other -1 values for micronutrients (potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are populated with plausible estimates cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 (Chicken breast, grilled) and ICMR-NIN grilled chicken data; only vitamin_d_mcg remains at -1 as it is genuinely not reported in the primary source. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Pudina Tikka was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. Sodium value of 380 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-style marination; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. The dish is served at Anjana Kitchen, Six Battery Road, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data (GD003014).
